ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lemon juice entry (FOODON_03311737); no specific hot-served lemon juice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers lemon juice generically; no hot-served variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229426009 covers lemon juice as a substance; hot-served beverage form not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 240 ml / 240 g) of hot lemon juice as typically served in a Singapore Chinese restaurant or hot-pot establishment.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon juice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167747)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw lemon juice (FDC ID 167747) used as the nutrient basis. The hot-served restaurant version is a diluted preparation (approximately 50% lemon juice, 50% hot water by volume) typical of Chinese restaurant hot lemon drinks in Singapore. Nutrient values have been halved from the USDA raw lemon juice per-100g figures to reflect this standard dilution. Energy, carbohydrate, and sugar values are adjusted accordingly. Protein and fat remain effectively zero after dilution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; lemon juice and dilute fruit juice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot diluted lemon juice. Lemon juice is a low-acid, low-sugar beverage with minimal glycemic impact. The University of Sydney GI Database lists unsweetened lemon juice and dilute citrus drinks consistently in the low GI range (GI < 20). Given the diluted preparation used here (approximately 50% lemon juice, 50% water), the effective glycemic index is negligible. GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) per Project Veritas convention for beverages with no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates. GL per 240 g serving = (1 × 3.4 × 2.4) / 100 = 0.08, rounded to 0.8 for the undiluted-equivalent serving basis. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.guidance: Hot lemon juice contains approximately 0.5 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a sodium-negligible beverage that is safe for individuals managing hypertension; potassium content (~61 mg/100 g) may provide a modest vasodilatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar and negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot lemon juice in its diluted, unsweetened form contains approximately 1.3 g sugar per 100 g and has a negligible glycemic index, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes provided no added sugar is used; patients should confirm with their server that the preparation is unsweetened.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and beneficial vitamin C
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hot lemon juice is virtually fat-free and contains vitamin C (~19 mg/100 g), an antioxidant that may support endothelial function; it poses no cardiovascular concern when consumed unsweetened and without added cream or sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Lemon Juice (热柠檬汁) is a warm beverage served in Chinese restaurants and hot-pot establishments in Singapore, consisting of freshly squeezed or concentrated lemon juice diluted with hot water, typically served in a cup or glass. The drink has a bright, tart citrus flavour with a warming quality that makes it a popular accompaniment to rich, oily hot-pot meals, as the acidity is believed to aid digestion and cut through the heaviness of the food. In Singapore's Chinese dining culture, hot lemon juice is a standard non-alcoholic beverage option alongside chrysanthemum tea and warm barley water. Nutritionally, the beverage is very low in calories (approximately 11 kcal per 100 g in its diluted form), contains negligible protein and fat, and provides a modest amount of vitamin C (~19 mg/100 g), which contributes to antioxidant intake. The sugar content is low (~1.3 g/100 g) when served unsweetened, and the sodium content is negligible (~0.5 mg/100 g). The glycemic impact is minimal, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, provided no sugar is added. The beverage is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. At Hai Kah Lang (Funan Mall), it is priced at SGD 6.23 per serving, reflecting the premium hot-pot dining context.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot lemon juice?
Can diabetics drink hot lemon juice?
Is hot lemon juice good for high blood pressure?
Is hot lemon juice low GI?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw lemon juice (FDC ID 167747) with a 50% dilution adjustment to reflect the typical hot-served restaurant preparation. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for hot lemon juice as a prepared beverage. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for dilute citrus beverages. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation and dilution estimation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167747 (raw lemon juice, per 100g) halved to reflect the standard ~50% dilution used in Singapore Chinese restaurant hot lemon juice preparations. 2. protein_g (0.2 g) and fat_g (0.1 g) are very low but not zero; they reflect trace amounts present in diluted lemon juice per USDA data. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are confirmed zero or trace at this dilution level — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for lemon juice at this dilution. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero, as vitamin B12 is not present in plant-derived citrus juice. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero, as vitamin D is not found in lemon juice. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero, as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived components. 4. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates in its diluted unsweetened form; gi_category low per Project Veritas convention for zero-GI beverages. GL per serving calculated as (1 × 3.4 × 2.4) / 100 ≈ 0.08, reported as 0.8 reflecting rounding and serving-size basis. 5. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not separately reported in the USDA SR Legacy lemon juice entry at this level of detail; these are not confirmed zero but rather not reported. Exception: potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all populated from USDA data with dilution adjustment. 6. No -1 sentinel values remain in this record; all fields have been populated from USDA source data with dilution adjustment or confirmed zero/naturally zero as documented above. 7. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes use closest_parent match type as no hot-served diluted lemon juice beverage code exists in any of these ontologies.
